coredata.dc:title: Excitation wavelength-tuned dual-color carbon dots as a dual-channel sensing system for fluorescent and visual detection of Mn (VII) and Al (III)
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Zhenhua
Du, Pan
Zhang, Quanxi
Fan, Xiaopeng
Zheng, Xianyun
Zhang, Yuexia
Dong, Chuan
coredata.dc:description: Heavy metal pollution is posing a serious threat to the environment. However, the multichannel fluorescence sensor simultaneously detecting multiple metals is promising but challenging. In this work, excitation-dependent red and blue carbon dots (RBCDs) were synthesized through carbonization and amide reaction. RBCDs emitted red and blue fluorescence under different excitation wavelengths. Interestingly, the fluorescence of RBCDs embedded in PVA films and various paper matrics was highly stable. The fluorescence emission of RBCDs at 590 nm was effectively quenched by MnO4 - via the oxidation of RBCDs and the inner filter effect. Instead, the fluorescence at 485 nm could be effectively quenched by morin, whereas Al3+ can reverse the quenching. Based on the interaction between RBCDs, MnO4 -, morin, and Al3+, dual-channel sensing systems for the analysis of MnO4 - and Al3+ were successfully constructed. The established sensing systems exhibited excellent selectivity and practical applicability for detecting MnO4 - and Al3+ in river water. These sensing systems offer several merits, such as low limits of detection (LOD), wide linear range, good anti-interference, and satisfactory detection reliability. These findings help to understand the behavior and distribution of these metal ions in the eco-environment, contributing to reinforcing resource management and strengthening environmental protection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-channel detection
Carbon dots
Permanganate
Aluminum

Fluorescence of the RBCDs embedded in PVA matrices
RBCDs@morin sensing systems for Al3+ detection
Cytotoxicity and bioimaging of RBCDs in Hela cells and zebrafish
Morphology and structural analysis of RBCDs
Fluorescence response to MnO4 - and sensing mechanism
Reliability of sensing systems for Mn(VII)/Al(III)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Synthesis route of RBCDs. (b) 3D emission spectra. (c) Emission spectra at the excitation of different wavelengths. (d-f) Fluorescence, CIE chromaticity diagram, and color purity of RBCDs embedded in PVA matrix. (g and h) Stability of RBCDs in various pH solutions and KCl. (i) Effect of irradiation time on the luminescence of as-prepared RBCDs.
(a) TEM images and size distribution of RBCDs. (b) FTIR spectrum of RBCDs. (c) XRD spectrum. (d) Full XPS, high-resolution C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s of RBCDs.

Detection platforms for MnO4 - detection. (a) Selective response of RBCDs toward various metal ions. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of RBCDs upon the gradual addition of MnO4 -. Inset: The plot between fluorescence quenching efficiency and MnO4 - concentration. (c) Visual detection system for MnO4 -. (d) Effect of RBCDs on cell viability. (e) Bioimaging of RBCDs with and without MnO4 - in Hela cell and zebrafish.

(a) Molecular configuration and molecular electrostatic potential diagram of RBCDs. (b) Absorption spectrum of MnO4 - and emission/excitation spectra of RBCDs. (c) Absorption spectra of RBCDs with and without MnO4 -. (d) Fitted lifetime of RBCDs with/without MnO4 -. (e) FTIR spectra of RBCDs before and after the addition of MnO4 -. (f) Cyclic voltammogram and (g) energy bands of RBCDs in the presence and absence of MnO4 -.

Based-RBCDs detecting system for Al3+. (a-d) Fluorescence, UV–Vis absorption spectra, zeta potential, and lifetime of RBCDs in the presence of morin and Al3+. (e) Anti-interference performance of the sensing system. (f) Fitted linear relationship between the fluorescence of RBCDs@morin and Al3+ concentration. (g-h) Photographs of visual detection and linear relationship between R/(G + B) and Al3+ concentration.
Detection of MnO4 - and Al3+ in river water.

head.ce:title: Excitation wavelength-tuned dual-color carbon dots as a dual-channel sensing system for fluorescent and visual detection of Mn (VII) and Al (III)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environmental and Resource Sciences, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 03006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental and Resource Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Environmental and Resource Sciences, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 03006, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Wucheng Road 92#, Taiyuan 030006, Shanxi Province, P.R. China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Heavy metal pollution is posing a serious threat to the environment. However, the multichannel fluorescence sensor simultaneously detecting multiple metals is promising but challenging. In this work, excitation-dependent red and blue carbon dots (RBCDs) were synthesized through carbonization and amide reaction. RBCDs emitted red and blue fluorescence under different excitation wavelengths. Interestingly, the fluorescence of RBCDs embedded in PVA films and various paper matrics was highly stable. The fluorescence emission of RBCDs at 590 nm was effectively quenched by MnO4 - via the oxidation of RBCDs and the inner filter effect. Instead, the fluorescence at 485 nm could be effectively quenched by morin, whereas Al3+ can reverse the quenching. Based on the interaction between RBCDs, MnO4 -, morin, and Al3+, dual-channel sensing systems for the analysis of MnO4 - and Al3+ were successfully constructed. The established sensing systems exhibited excellent selectivity and practical applicability for detecting MnO4 - and Al3+ in river water. These sensing systems offer several merits, such as low limits of detection (LOD), wide linear range, good anti-interference, and satisfactory detection reliability. These findings help to understand the behavior and distribution of these metal ions in the eco-environment, contributing to reinforcing resource management and strengthening environmental protection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal pollution is increasingly becoming a great concern with rapid industrialization, including metallurgy, dying, printing, leather, pharmaceutical, and mining due to non-biodegradable properties[1–3]. The heavy metals released into the environment, especially permanganate (MnO4 -) and aluminum, pose a threat to people’s health and ecosystem[4–6]. MnO4 - is widely used as a disinfectant owing to its strong bactericidal ability to oxidize the related genes in bacteria[7]. However, long-term exposure to MnO4 - causes respiratory damage, skin injury, diarrhea, and vomiting[8]. Therefore, excessive MnO4 - levels in groundwater threaten human health owing to its toxicity, bioaccumulation, persistence, and potential hazard[9,10]. Apart from MnO4 -, the presence of excessive Al3+ levels can bring unforeseen hazards. Al is the most abundant metallic element on earth and is widely used in vehicle production, pharmaceutical items, hygiene products, vaccines, antacids, and food industry[11]. Excessive Al levels are closely linked to brain damage such as Parkinson’s disease and Alzheimer's disease[12]. The maximum permissible levels of Al and Mn ions in drinking water are 7.41 and 1.82 μM[3]. Therefore, accurate and sensitive detection of MnO4 - and Al3+ ions is crucial for ensuring safe drinking water and hence safeguarding public health.

Many methods have been developed for the detection of MnO4 - and Al3+ [13–17]. However, some detection methods require time-consuming synthetic procedures, complex instrumental techniques, and specific pretreatment to improve the selectivity. Although these fluorescence detecting systems usually show superior features of high sensitivity, excellent specificity, and visualization recognition, most of the above fluorescent sensors, as one single output signal, can detect one metal[18–22]. It is necessary to design a multi-channel fluorescent probe that is convenient, fast, sensitive, and environmentally friendly to simultaneously detect MnO4 - and Al3+. Carbon dots (CDs) as advanced carbon nanomaterial have been widely used in fluorescence sensing, biological imaging, and photoelectric fields[5,23]. However, the majority of CDs emit blue or green fluorescence, and a few CDs were found to emit red fluorescence[24]. Multicolor CDs emitting distinct fluorescence can achieve simultaneous detection of multiple analytes[24–26]. Long-wavelength multicolor CDs minimize autofluorescence errors and provide visual color changes for target analyte visualization in sensing applications[27]. Therefore, multicolor CDs are promising nanomaterials in multi-target sensing.

Red CDs have strong tissue penetrability and are less affected by the autofluorescence of bio-samples[28,29]. Previous studies show that the structure and optical properties of CDs can be tuned through the structure of precursors and various doped elements[30–33]. Accordingly, choosing a large conjugated molecule and/or doping heteroatoms as precursors is a powerful approach to tuning the luminescence behavior of CDs for achieving the sensing. An organic molecule containing amine and aromatic rings is expected to be an excellent precursor for the synthesis of long-wavelength CDs with highly conjugated π systems with narrow band gaps[34]. Safranine containing benzo and amino groups is a promising raw material to produce long-wavelength CDs. Safranine-derived CDs via solvothermal method were applied in the detection of endogenous/exogenous peroxynitrite[35]. Jiao et al. synthesized safranine-derived CDs using phosphoric acid as a dopant via hydrothermal method for hypochlorous detection[36]. These safranine-derived CDs have the same emission peak under different excitation wavelengths, which limits the sensing application for multiple targets.

Since the amide group contributes to the electron transfer of metal-bridged CDs[37], the amide reaction between amino and carboxyl groups is also conducive to introducing N groups into CDs to achieve multicolor CDs to obtain good selectivity for metals. Inspired by this design idea, we believe that the prepared CDs using safranine and oxalic acid as precursors might emit multicolor fluorescence owing to abundant N groups and carboxyl of CDs, which renders them easy to chelate or react with different species of metal ions.

In this study, wavelength-tunable red and blue carbon dots (RBCDs) were synthesized using safranine with a rich conjugated structure. The emission spectra of RBCDs can split into two peaks under different light irradiation that emit red fluorescence and blue emission. The peak at 590 nm exhibited the sensing features for MnO4 -. Instead, another peak of RBCDs was considered as a fluorescence signal to detect Al3+ using morin as a bridge. Morin, a member of the flavanol class, serves as a beneficial agent in many dietary supplements and pharmaceutical preparations to inhibit the progression of many diseases and improve human health [38]. Herein, morin is combined with RBCDs to fabricate the visual and fluorescent sensing system for Al3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the RBCDs
ce:section.ce:para: RBCDs were prepared via a hydrothermal method. Briefly, 25 mg safranine and 5 mg oxalic acid were dispersed in 20 mL of purified water by ultrasonicating. Subsequently, the mixture was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and heated up to 200 °C for 8 h. The resulting mixture was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane, followed by dialyzing against distilled water for 48 h. After freeze-drying, the RBCD powder was stored at 4 °C until further utilization. All the reagents and the characterization of the prepared RBCDs are provided in the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence of the RBCDs embedded in PVA matrices
ce:section.ce:para: The RBCDs were loaded into PVA matrices by a simple method[39]. 10 % (m/m) PVA solution was prepared by stirring vigorously at 85 °C until complete dissolution. To obtain uniform RBCDs, the PVA solution was thoroughly mixed with a certain amount of RBCDs solution and then heated at 60 °C for 24 h. Finally, the RBCDs were successfully loaded onto PVA matrices.

In a typical experiment, RBCDs were mixed with different concentrations of MnO4 - solution. The emission spectra of RBCDs at 590 nm were recorded with optimal excitation wavelength in the presence and absence of MnO4 -. For the selectivity experiment, 10 μL of 0.1 M metal ions were separately added to RBCDs solutions and then the fluorescence change of RBCDs was evaluated. Visual detection was achieved by capturing the picture of the sensing system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: RBCDs@morin sensing systems for Al3+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: The recognition of Al3+ was investigated in aqueous RBCDs solution at pH = 7.4 at ambient temperature. In detail, 0.1 mg/mL RBCDs solution was added to 2 mL of PBS solution, followed by the addition of 0.1 mM morin. The fluorescence spectra were recorded. When the fluorescence was quenched to about 10 %, the platform was successfully constructed for Al3+ detection. To evaluate the selectivity of the sensing system, other metal ions were considered as co-existing ions. The fluorescence spectrum at 485 nm was collected. The colors of the RBCDs@morin with Al3+ at various concentrations were converted into R, G, and B values so that an efficient visual sensing system was fabricated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity and bioimaging of RBCDs in Hela cells and zebrafish
ce:section.ce:para: Cytotoxicity of the RBCDs was determined using an MTT assay. Briefly, Hela cells (1 × 104 cells per well) were incubated in DMEM supplied with 10 % FBS for 24 h. Then, the culture medium was replaced with different concentrations of RBCDs solution for 30 min at 37 °C. The analysis of cell viability was performed according to the previous report[40].

For imaging, the cells were treated with 0.02 mg/mL RBCDs solution for 30 min at 37 °C. Subsequently, the cells were washed three times with fresh pH = 7.4 phosphate buffer solution to remove the remaining RBCDs, followed by incubation with 0.1 mM MnO4 - for 30 min at 37 °C under a humid atmosphere of 5 % CO2, respectively. Cellular fluorescence images were recorded by laser scanning confocal microscope (LSCM, Zeiss, Germany). Furthermore, wild zebrafish at 4 d post-fertilization were incubated at 28 °C with E3 medium containing 0.02 mg/mL RBCDs for 1 h. The zebrafish were then washed three times with fresh E3 nutrition solution to remove any remaining RBCDs. Subsequently, the zebrafish were exposed to 0.10 mM MnO4 - for 1 h. Images were acquired using a LSCM.

RBCDs were synthesized from safranine and oxalic acid as precursors via a hydrothermal method (Fig. 1 a). The UV–Vis absorption of the as-fabricated RBCDs has three peaks at 248, 276, and 520 nm (Fig. S1). The former two peaks are assigned to π-π* transitions[15]. The absorption band at 520 nm is attributed to n-π* transitions of the surface states containing C=O/C=N functional groups[35]. The RBCDs possess a maximum emission wavelength of 590 nm and have a narrow full width at half maximum of 68.4 nm (Fig. 1b). The prepared RBCDs emit blue and red fluorescence excited from 315 to 555 nm, respectively (Fig. 1c). The phenomenon might be attributed to the interplay of quantum effects, surface states, and/or chemical composition[41]. The multicolor carbon nanomaterials are promising for the development of a multi-target probe to simultaneously detect metal pollutants in the environment. The quantum yield of the RBCDs was 5.13 % when using cresol purple as a reference, which is lower than that reported derived from phosphoric acid and safranine[36]. The red fluorescence of RBCDs embedded in the PVA matrix exhibits a dose-dependent increase, which is similar to that in an aqueous solution (Fig. 1d and 1e). Different concentrations of RBCDs dispersed into solid matrics including filter, no fluorescent, and stone displayed orange fluorescence under UV light (Fig. S2-S4). Instead, the RBCDs did not exhibit any color under daylight. The written patterns were unchanged in ambient conditions for 5 weeks due to the excellent stability of the RBCDs (Fig. S5).

Furthermore, the color purity of the OCDs in PVA matrices was calculated (Supporting Information). The levels of color purity of RBCDs are enhanced as RBCD concentration increases (Fig. 1f). The stability of these RBCDs was examined under different challenges including pH, KCl solution, and continuous irradiation. Excellent stability of RBCDs was observed in various circumstances including ionic concentrations, pH values, and irradiation time (Fig. 1g-1i). These optical properties make them good candidates for sensing applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structural analysis of RBCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Considering that the optical properties of nanomaterials mainly depend on their structure, the RBCDs were characterized with transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS). The TEM images confirm the formation of nearly monodisperse RBCDs with an average size of approximately 1.87 nm (Fig. 2 a). HRTEM results show that no crystal lattice fringes are observed. As shown in Fig. 2b, the peak at 3432 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of N–H/O–H. A couple of absorption peaks at 1614 and 1639 cm−1 correspond to amide linkage (–CONH–)[42], strongly proving the dehydration condensation of safranine and oxalic acid during the formation of RBCDs. The functional groups on the surface of the RBCDs, including C=N (1530 cm−1), C=C (1500 cm−1), C–N (1385 cm−1), and C–O (1148 cm−1), are observed[42]. The XRD spectrum in Fig. 2c exhibits a wide and weak diffraction peak around 25◦, suggesting an amorphous structure of the prepared RBCDs. Furthermore, three peaks at C1s 284.8, N1s 399.2, and O1s 532.1 eV in the full XPS spectrum reveal that the RBCDs consist of C, N, and O elements, which were calculated to be 78.99 %, 12.34 %, 8.67 %, respectively. In Fig. 2d, the high-resolution XPS spectrum of the C 1 s was fitted into three peaks at 284.8, 285.7, and 288.8 eV, which are ascribed to C–C/C=C, C–N/C–O, and C=O chemical bonds[17]. Three peaks in the N 1 s spectrum correspond to pyridinic N (398.9), amino N (399.4 eV), and pyrrolic N (400.7 eV)[43]. The O 1s spectrum indicates the existence of the C=O (531.9 eV) C–O (532.7 eV)[44]. The large-conjugation domain inherited from the raw materials and rich electron-donating/electron-withdrawing groups (–OH, –NH −C-O, and −C=O) may contribute to the dual-color fluorescence of RBCDs[45]. The hydrophilic groups of the obtained RBCDs broaden their application in various environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response to MnO4 - and sensing mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: RBCDs can be explored as a potential sensing system due to their excellent optical features. To estimate the selectivity and specificity of the RBCDs-based sensing system for MnO4 -, the photoluminescence signals of RBCDs with different metal ions were recorded under the same conditions. The change of fluorescence intensity was negligible, except for MnO4 -, suggesting an excellent selectivity for MnO4 - (Fig. 3 a). Furthermore, red fluorescence of the sensing system at 590 nm gradually declined with the increase of MnO4 - from 0 to 1000 μM (Fig. 3b and Fig. S6). A good linear relationship was observed in the range of 0–400 μM. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.227 μM. The sensing system is comparable to the previously reported sensors[2,4,46]. Although some detection systems possess low LOD[5,16], their synthesis process is either cost-consuming or emits blue fluorescence, which limits their application in biosystems.

Inspired by the quenching efficiency of RBCDs by metal ions, the potential application in the anti-counterfeiting field was explored on various matrices including filter paper, no fluorescence paper, and written stone paper using RBCDs or RBCDs@MnO4 - solutions using a commonly used pen. As shown in Fig. S7, the color of the written patterns using RBCD solution is almost invisible compared with those using RBCD solution under UV light. The feature broadens their application in anti-counterfeiting field[39].

Furthermore, RBCDs were explored as a visual sensing system for MnO4 - by analyzing their color changes. Interestingly, the color of RBCDs with MnO4 - changed to pale pink (Fig. 3c). A linear relationship was established by plotting R2/(G + B) against MnO4 - concentration between 5 and 300 μM. Therefore, a smartphone-assisted visual detection system for MnO4 - was successfully developed for real-time monitoring of MnO4 - content with an LOD of 1.58 μM, which is higher than the above-fabricated fluorescent sensing system.

To examine the biocompatibility of RBCDs, HeLa cells were incubated with different concentrations of RBCDs. As shown in Fig. 3d, the cell survival rate was higher than 80 % by MTT assay. The low cytotoxicity of RBCDs prompts us to investigate their application in vivo. Zebrafish and Hela cells were chosen as model organisms, which provides reliable results[47]. As shown in Fig. 3e, 0.1 mM of MnO4 - quenched the fluorescence of RBCDs while RBCDs emitting orange fluorescence can penetrate Hela cells and zebrafish. These results provide comprehensive evidence of the applicability of a smartphone-assisted visible and fluorescence dual-mode sensing system for fast, sensitive, immediate, and portable quantitative detection of MnO4 - content within a wide range.

To elucidate the binding mechanism of the RBCDs to MnO4 -, the interaction of RBCDs with MnO4 - was verified by spectral analysis, fluorescence lifetime, density functional theory (DFT) calculations with B3LYP/6––311 G*, FTIR, and cyclic voltammetry. The molecular electrostatic potential diagram represents an electron-rich area[48]. The average local ionization energy of RBCDs centers at sites 16 N and 40 N (Fig. 4 a). Fluorescence quenching typically results from static and dynamic [49]. Dynamic quenching occurs through electron and fluorescence resonance energy transfer. A large overlap was observed between the absorption band of MnO4 - and the fluorescence spectra of the RBCDs (Fig. 4b), suggesting that resonance energy or electron transfer might account for fluorescence quenching in the sensing platform. Additionally, UV–vis absorption spectra of the RBCDs in the absence and presence of MnO4 - were unchanged (Fig. 4c), ruling out a possibility of static quenching. The RBCDs and RBCDs/morin fluorescence delays are depicted in Fig. 4d. Notably, the decreased lifetime of SNCDs with the addition of MnO4 - confirms that the quenching process results from dynamic quenching. From the FTIR spectra before and after the addition of MnO4 - (Fig. 4e), the disappearance of the Ar-N–H peak at 3189 cm−1 and the change in the peak at 1540 cm−1 were accompanied by a decrease in the peak at 1637 cm−1 and the formation of a new peak at 1407 cm−1. These changes in FTIR combined with the DFT result suggest the occurrence of a redox reaction between the amino group in the benzene ring with MnO4 - to form a nitro group, consequently leading to fluorescence quenching of RBCDs.

The energy levels of the RBCDs were determined from electrochemical studies [50]. Fig. 4f exhibits the reduction and oxidation peaks of RBCDs alone are −0.609 V and −0.511 V, while those of RBCDs with MnO4 - are −0.637 V and −0.394 V, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4g, The LUMO and HOMO energy levels are calculated to be 0.098 and 0.243 eV according to the empirical formula ELUMO = − e(Ered + 4.4) and EHOMO = − e(Eox + 4.4), which results in the fluorescence quenching of RBCDs.

Since Al3+ released into water environment threatens human health, it is of key importance to develop a facile and accurate method for the quantitative determination of Al3+ [6,13]. Ma et al. reported that the N-doped CDs from citric acid and o-phenylenediamine sequentially determine morin and Al3+ [21]. This prompts us to fabricate an RBCDs@morin platform to determine Al3+. Fortunately, morin dose-dependently quenched the fluorescence of RBCDs (Fig. S8), which can be recovered by the addition of Al3+ (Fig. 5 a). Therefore, morin can be utilized to combine with the RBCDs to develop an on–off-on sensing system for Al3+ detection. To verify the mechanism of the detection scheme, the corresponding fluorescence spectra, UV–Vis absorption, zeta potential, and lifetime of RBCDs and RBCDs@morin with or without Al3+ were examined. As shown in Fig. 5b, a new peak at 413 nm in the absorption spectra was observed when Al3+ was added to the RBCDs@morin system, indicativing the formation of a new complex. Furthermore, the opposite charges of RBCDs@morin and Al3+ (Fig. 5c) result in an electrostatic attraction between them. These findings indicate that there is a strong interaction between Al3+ and RBCDs@morin so that Al3+ detection can be realized[15]. The fluorescence lifetimes of RBCDs, RBCDs@morin, and RBCDs@morin@Al3+ followed a double exponential formula, which could be attributed to the recombination of the carbon core and surface state, respectively[51]. The decrease in the average lifetimes of RBCDs, RBCDs@morin, and RBCDs@morin@Al3+ (6.59, 5.61, and 4.09 ns) shows a possibility of electron transfer between Al3+ and morin (Fig. 5d). Therefore, the possible reason for the fluorescence recovery of the RBCDs-morin system is the strong chelation interaction between morin and Al3+ make the collapse of RBCDs@Morin structure, eventually leading to the recovery and redshift of the quenched red fluorescence[21].

Next, the selectivity and anti-interference of the RBCDs@morin sensing system were explored by introducing other metal ions. The results show that the RBCDs@morin sensing system has a good sensing ability for Al3+ detection (Fig. 5e). The quenched fluorescence by morin can be recovered by Al3+ and exhibited a good linear relationship in the range of 0.5–5.0 μM with the LOD of 0.113 μM, which is far below the WHO recommended limit of 7.4 μM for Al3+ in drinking water. Compared with the previous studies[17,22], the sensing system has a low LOD value, which broadens its application in various environments.

Visual detection is a convenient method for detecting metal ions. There was an excellent correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.9898) between the fluorescence enhancement efficiency and Al3+ concentrations ranging from 0.5 to 10.0 μM. Interestingly, when Al3+ was added to the RBCDs solution, obvious color changes were observed under daylight (Fig. 5g), demonstrating the practicability of the visual platform based on RBCDs, which further amplifies the application scope of the RBCDs. The assay had a good linear relationship in the range of 0.5–10.0 μM (Fig. 5h).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reliability of sensing systems for Mn(VII)/Al(III)
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the accuracy and reliability of the sensing system for MnO4 -/Al3+ detection under real conditions, the proposed sensing platforms were used for the detection of MnO4 -/Al3+ in real water samples (Table 1 ).

Quantitative recoveries of 103.66 %, 93.30 %, and 98.63 % were obtained for the real water samples spiked with 20.00, 50.00, and 100.00 μM of MnO4 -, respectively, demonstrating the reliability and potential practicability of the developed fluorescence sensing system. The practicability of the assay for Al3+ detection shows a range of 97.69–102.27 % in river water. The corresponding RSD values suggested the reliability and precision of the sensing systems for determining MnO4 -/Al3+ in real samples.

The excitation-dependent dual-color RBCDs were synthesized using containing N aromatic compound and oxalic acid via hydrothermal and amide reaction. The fluorescence of RBCDs in aqueous solution and solid matrices possesses excellent stability under various environments. Thanks to their excellent optical features and excitation-tuning fluorescence, the RBCDs-based optical and visual sensing systems were constructed through different manners, and their sensing performance was exploited. The RBCDs can simultaneously detect dual-target using blue and red fluorescence to avoid the interference of the single-fluorescence nanomaterials in determining multiple metal ions.
Funding

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 42477031), the Shanxi Scholarship Council of China (2023–030) and the Applied Basic Research Project of Shanxi Province (20210302123472).

Zhenhua Yang: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Formal analysis. Pan Du: Investigation, Data curation. Quanxi Zhang: Funding acquisition. Xiaopeng Fan: Investigation, Data curation. Xianyun Zheng: Funding acquisition. Yuexia Zhang: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Conceptualization. Chuan Dong: Supervision, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2025.163019.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Improving capacity for phytoremediation of Vetiver grass and Indian mustard in heavy metal (Al and Mn) contaminated water through the application of clay minerals
Environ. Sci. Pollut. Res.,

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B.O. Otunola, M.P. Aghoghovwia, M. Thwala, A. Gómez-Arias, R. Jordaan, J.C. Hernandez, O.O. Ololade, Improving capacity for phytoremediation of Vetiver grass and Indian mustard in heavy metal (Al and Mn) contaminated water through the application of clay minerals, Environ. Sci. Pollut. Res., 30 (2023) 53577-53588.
